passages.title: Sweet Work: How Sap Becomes Syrup
passages.subject: ELA
passages.text:
Every year in late winter, the Alvarez family walks into a quiet woods carrying a drill, a handful of metal spouts, and clean buckets. The maple trees stand bare and still, as if they are asleep. But deep inside each trunk, something is already beginning to move. In a few weeks, these same trees will help fill jars with golden maple syrup. Turning thin, watery sap into thick, sweet syrup is slow work. It takes patience, the right weather, and a great deal of boiling. Most people only ever see the finished syrup on their plate, never the weeks of work behind it.

### Waiting for the Right Weather
Sugar maple trees store a kind of sugar-water called sap inside their trunks and roots. Sap only flows well for a short season, usually during late February and March. The trees need a very particular pattern of weather: freezing nights followed by warmer days above freezing. On freezing nights, pressure changes help draw water into the tree. When the next day warms above freezing, pressure builds in the wood and pushes the sap back outward. If the weather stays icy all day, the sap barely moves. If it turns warm and stays warm too soon, the season ends almost before it starts. That is why syrup makers watch the thermometer almost as carefully as they watch the trees.

### Tapping the Trees
When the weather is finally right, the Alvarez family drills a small hole into each healthy maple. Into the hole they gently tap a metal spout, called a spile.¹ A bucket or a long plastic tube hangs below to catch the steady drips. The sap that comes out looks almost exactly like water. It is only a little sweet, because it is mostly water with just a small amount of sugar mixed in. A single tree might drip many gallons over the whole season, yet it is never harmed. The hole is small, and the tree heals it by the following year. A wide, older maple can hold more than one spile, but thin young trees are left alone so they can keep growing.

### Boiling It Down
Collecting the sap is only the beginning. To become syrup, sap must be boiled for hours inside a building called a sugarhouse. As the sap heats, the water in it turns into steam and floats away into the air. This slow change is called evaporation.² Hour after hour, the watery sap grows thicker, darker, and much sweeter. The amount of boiling is hard to believe: it takes about forty gallons of thin sap to make a single gallon of finished syrup. That is one reason real maple syrup costs more than syrup made from plain corn sugar. Someone must keep the fire steady and watch the bubbling sap the entire time, because syrup that boils a few minutes too long can scorch and turn bitter.

### From Tree to Table
When the syrup finally reaches just the right thickness, it is poured through a filter to remove tiny bits of bark, then sealed into clean jars while it is still warm. The Alvarez family keeps some for their own pancakes and sells the rest at a spring farmers market. By the time the jars are filled, the maples have begun to grow tiny green buds, and the sap stops flowing for the year. The work is finished. But next winter, when the nights turn cold and the days slowly warm again, the family will return to the same quiet woods and begin the sweet, slow work all over again.

> **Did You Know?** A sugar maple can live for more than two hundred years — long enough for many generations of families to tap it.

¹ **spile** — a small spout that carries sap out of a tree
² **evaporation** — when a liquid slowly turns into a gas, the way water turns into steam

factCheckNotesJson.claim: Sap flows by a freeze-thaw pressure cycle: below-freezing draws water in; above-freezing pushes sap out.
Freeze-thaw pressure generation occurs in sugar maple.
factCheckNotesJson.sourceTitle: Freeze-thaw-induced pressure generation in red and sugar maple

factCheckNotesJson.claim: About forty gallons of sap make one gallon of syrup, with variation by sugar content.
factCheckNotesJson.sourceTitle: How Much Sap Can One Tree Produce
factCheckNotesJson.organization: New York State Maple Producers Association

factCheckNotesJson.claim: A sugar maple can live for more than two hundred years.
factCheckNotesJson.sourceTitle: Silvics of North America, Vol. 2 — Acer saccharum (Sugar Maple)

factCheckNotesJson.claim: A spile is a spout that carries sap out of the tree.
factCheckNotesJson.sourceTitle: How to Tap and Make Maple Syrup

items.subject: ELA
items.studentFacingPrompt: What is the passage mostly about?
answerChoicesJson: The maples grow tiny green buds when the season ends.
Making syrup needs right weather, careful tapping, and boiling.
The Alvarez family sells syrup at a spring farmers market.
Maple syrup is healthier than corn syrup.
distractorRationalesJson: Too narrow: this ending detail does not state what the whole passage is mostly about.
Whole-passage synthesis: the answer draws across the process sections rather than one literal sentence.
Too narrow: the market detail appears at the end but does not cover the process.
Unsupported inference: the passage compares cost, not health.

items.subject: ELA
items.studentFacingPrompt: What does scorch mean as it is used in the passage?
distractorRationalesJson: Opposite claim: the context is boiling, not cooling.
Plausible misreading: the passage mentions liquid sap and syrup, but scorch means burn.
Wrong emphasis: syrup thickens during boiling, but scorch means burn.

structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Opposite claim: the context is boiling, not cooling.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: Someone must keep the fire steady and watch the bubbling sap the entire time, because syrup that boils a few minutes too long can scorch and turn bitter.

structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Plausible misreading: the passage mentions liquid sap and syrup, but scorch means burn.

items.subject: ELA
items.studentFacingPrompt: Why does sap flow best when the nights are freezing and the days are warm?
answerChoicesJson: The warm days make the maples grow new buds.
The cold nights make the sap taste sweeter than daytime sap.
The changing weather fills the buckets with rain.
Freezing nights and warm days build pressure that helps sap flow.

structuredChoicesJson.text: The warm days make the maples grow new buds.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Wrong section: green buds appear at the season's end, not as the reason sap flows best.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: By the time the jars are filled, the maples have begun to grow tiny green buds, and the sap stops flowing for the year.

structuredChoicesJson.text: The cold nights make the sap taste sweeter than daytime sap.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Unsupported inference: the passage describes pressure changes, not different sweetness by time of day.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: The trees need a very particular pattern of weather: freezing nights followed by warmer days above freezing.

structuredChoicesJson.text: The changing weather fills the buckets with rain.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Unsupported inference: buckets collect sap, not rain.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: A bucket or a long plastic tube hangs below to catch the steady drips.

structuredChoicesJson.text: Freezing nights and warm days build pressure that helps sap flow.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Correct: the weather pattern creates pressure changes that help move sap.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: When the next day warms above freezing, pressure builds in the wood and pushes the sap back outward.

items.subject: ELA
items.studentFacingPrompt: How does the "Did You Know?" box add to the passage?
answerChoicesJson: It shows that a sugar maple can live for many generations.
It explains how the syrup is poured through a filter into jars.
It tells why thin young trees are left alone.
It proves that syrup is healthier than corn syrup.

structuredChoicesJson.text: It shows that a sugar maple can live for many generations.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Correct: the sidebar gives extra information about how long sugar maples can live.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: A sugar maple can live for more than two hundred years — long enough for many generations of families to tap it.

structuredChoicesJson.text: It explains how the syrup is poured through a filter into jars.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Wrong section: pouring syrup through a filter into jars is explained in the main passage, not in the "Did You Know?" box.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: When the syrup finally reaches just the right thickness, it is poured through a filter to remove tiny bits of bark, then sealed into clean jars while it is still warm.

structuredChoicesJson.text: It tells why thin young trees are left alone.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Wrong section: young trees are discussed in the tapping section, not the sidebar.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: A wide, older maple can hold more than one spile, but thin young trees are left alone so they can keep growing.

structuredChoicesJson.text: It proves that syrup is healthier than corn syrup.
structuredChoicesJson.rationale: Unsupported inference: the passage compares cost, not health.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: That is one reason real maple syrup costs more than syrup made from plain corn sugar.

partA.stem: Why do syrup makers need both the right weather and careful boiling?

choices.text: The weather makes the syrup turn golden before it leaves the tree.
Boiling helps the trees grow buds before winter begins.
Weather helps sap flow, and boiling turns sap into syrup.
The cold nights make syrup pour through a filter into jars.

items.passageTitle: Sweet Work: How Sap Becomes Syrup
items.stem: Explain how sap changes into syrup. Use two details from the passage to support your answer.
items.studentFacingPrompt: Explain how sap changes into syrup. Use two details from the passage to support your answer.
items.instructionText: Write a short answer that uses two details from the passage to support your answer.
items.expectedAnswerCore: Sap starts as thin sugar-water. The right weather helps it flow from the tree, and boiling removes water so it becomes thicker and sweeter.

acceptableTextSupport.quotedSpan: The trees need a very particular pattern of weather: freezing nights followed by warmer days above freezing.
acceptableTextSupport.detail: freezing nights followed by warmer days help sap flow
acceptableTextSupport.connectsToExpectedAnswer: This supports the weather part of the process.

acceptableTextSupport.quotedSpan: As the sap heats, the water in it turns into steam and floats away into the air.
acceptableTextSupport.connectsToExpectedAnswer: This supports how sap thickens into syrup.

rubric.points3: Explains the process clearly and uses two details (freeze/warm weather and boiling/evaporation).
rubric.points2: Mostly correct explanation with one clear support.
rubric.points1: One copied or partial detail with little explanation.

scoreBandExamples.response: Sap is thin and watery when it leaves the tree. Freezing nights and warm days help the sap flow, and boiling it for a long time makes the water turn to steam and leave, so the sap gets thick and sweet.
scoreBandExamples.why: Explains the process and uses two details (weather-driven flow + boiling/evaporation).

scoreBandExamples.response: The sap is boiled for a long time until the water leaves and it turns into syrup.
scoreBandExamples.why: Mostly correct but uses one detail (boiling), not the weather.
One partial detail with little explanation of the process.

scoreBandExamples.response: Maple syrup is sweet and good on pancakes.
scoreBandExamples.why: Unsupported / unrelated to how sap becomes syrup.

items.instructionText: Drag each step into the correct order.

tokens.text: The sap is boiled so the water turns to steam and floats away.
tokens.evidenceQuote: As the sap heats, the water in it turns into steam and floats away into the air.
tokens.rationale: The passage explains that heating the sap makes water turn to steam, so this belongs second.

tokens.text: The finished syrup is poured through a filter and sealed in jars.
tokens.evidenceQuote: it is poured through a filter to remove tiny bits of bark, then sealed into clean jars
tokens.rationale: The passage describes filtering and sealing after the syrup is ready, so this belongs third.

tokens.text: A bucket catches the sap as it drips from the tapped tree.
tokens.evidenceQuote: A bucket or a long plastic tube hangs below to catch the steady drips.
tokens.rationale: The passage begins the process with sap dripping from the tapped tree, so this belongs first.
